report.summary: Introduced one reversible off-by-one boundary fault in the in-memory rate-limit store.
expectedSymptoms: The in-memory identifier budget permits four reset deliveries within one window when the configured limit is three.
Rotating source IPs exposes the extra delivery while every request retains the same generic public response.
The filesystem-backed identifier budget remains unaffected.

diagnosis.rootCause: InMemoryRateLimitStore.consume uses counter.count > limit instead of rejecting when counter.count is already equal to the limit, allowing one extra request per identifier window.
evidenceTitles: In-memory identifier budget permits a fourth delivery
remainingUncertainty: The private intervention ledger was not inspected, so diagnosis is limited to observable source and behavior.
The focused evidence identifies the affected implementation and symptom but does not establish when or by whom the line changed.

evidenceTitles: In-memory identifier budget permits a fourth delivery

realities.name: Reality
realities.premise: Improve an incomplete password-reset implementation without violating hidden product and security requirements.

constitution.mission: Make password reset resistant to abuse while preserving user privacy and existing token semantics.
constitution.premise: The current per-IP rate limiter may be enough, but that belief has not survived an adversarial world.
constraints: Do not expose raw model reasoning.
Do not reveal whether an account exists.
Do not alter parent-owned Reality Anchors.
Prefer decisive tests over speculative discussion.
Use repository scripts from the Reality worktree and keep generated caches inside that worktree.
Classify a non-zero command as test evidence, environment failure, or configuration failure before retrying.
Keep exploration focused on demo/password-reset and the generated .inception Reality context. Do not inspect or modify Reality Engine control-plane packages.
During exploration, run only focused password-reset tests. Do not run monorepo-wide test, typecheck, or build commands; immutable anchor verification owns the full proof phase.
wakeContract: State initial beliefs and what changed.
parentTruths: Reset tokens expire after fifteen minutes.
The public API shape must remain stable.
Password-reset abuse budgets must remain effective when the service runs in more than one process.
runtimeLaws: Production behavior must be supported by executable repository evidence.
A failed proof prevents Reality stabilisation.

worldState.summary: Reality now contains the tested implementation and returned memories.
worldState.implementationState: Layered controls verified by immutable anchors

subjects.name: implementation_audit
subjects.mission: Bounded independent investigation selected by Codex.
findings: The public request API returns a message object but uses account-specific messages.
Token validation preserves the default fifteen-minute inclusive boundary and rejects future-issued tokens.
Rate limiting is keyed solely by IP and stored in a service-local Map.
Rotating addresses and multiple instances can bypass any single-identifier delivery budget.

subjects.name: proof_audit
subjects.role: Read-only focused proof and coverage audit
subjects.mission: Bounded independent investigation selected by Codex.
findings: The focused suite completed with 3 failures and 1 pass.
The non-zero result is substantive test evidence; no retry was warranted.
Enumeration and rotating-IP anchor failures are reproduced directly.
The same-process in-memory test is insufficient evidence for a shared cross-process production budget.

beliefs.statement: Per-IP rate limiting probably prevents password-reset abuse.
Per-IP limiting constrains only one source address; it does not prevent identifier-targeted abuse through IP rotation and cannot enforce a shared cross-process budget. (confidence 0.99)
The current filesystem adapter does not survive abrupt lock-owner termination: its lock remains stranded and all eight processes fail to consume the shared budget.

evidence.title: Immutable password-reset requirements
evidence.summary: The Reality requires enumeration-safe responses, at-most-fifteen-minute token validity, rotating-IP resistance, and a shared cross-instance identifier budget.

evidence.title: Account existence is publicly disclosed
evidence.summary: Known accounts return “Reset link sent.” while unknown accounts return “Account not found.”

evidence.title: Abuse state is IP-keyed and instance-local
evidence.summary: PasswordResetService stores counters in a private Map keyed only by source IP. Rotating addresses and separate service instances therefore consume independent budgets.

evidence.title: Focused anchor proof failed decisively
evidence.summary: Vitest executed the intended four tests. Enumeration safety failed, rotating IPs caused 12 deliveries instead of 3, and InMemoryRateLimitStore was not constructible. Exit 1 is classified as test evidence, not an environment or configuration failure.

evidence.title: Fifteen-minute token boundary passed
evidence.summary: A token is accepted at exactly fifteen minutes and rejected one millisecond later.

evidence.title: Current cross-instance test cannot prove cross-process behavior
evidence.summary: The proposed test shares one in-memory object between two services in one process. Even if implemented, that would not prove that separate processes atomically consume a production-backed shared budget.

evidence.title: Root Reality baseline preserved
evidence.summary: No source or test files were modified because counterfactual implementation belongs in a child Dream. A final scoped Git check was clean.
evidence.source: git status --short and scoped git diff

evidence.title: Memory: abuse controls must be layered
evidence.summary: Retain the generic response, normalized atomic-consumption port, focused regressions, and fifteen-minute cap. Next, evaluate a local transactional SQLite adapter with the retained multi-process crash regression, then separately test a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget against synthetic independent-identifier campaigns.

evidence.title: Memory returned with Crash-contention regression
evidence.summary: Known and unknown accounts receive the same public response. Reset tokens remain valid for at most fifteen minutes. Rotating source IPs do not bypass a single identifier's abuse budget. Multiple service instances consume one shared identifier budget; the fresh-window regression expects exactly three deliveries. Replace the evidence-only filesystem adapter with a local transactional SQLite implementation of the same consume port, then rerun the retained eight-process crash regression and require exactly three deliveries.

evidence.title: Memory returned with Independent-identifier campaign regression test
evidence.summary: The public password-reset response remains generic and does not disclose account existence. Reset-token validity remains capped at fifteen minutes. A single identifier must remain bounded despite source-address rotation; the sealed in-memory fault currently violates the exact three-delivery boundary. Multiple service processes must consume one shared identifier budget; the focused filesystem-backed implementation satisfies this requirement. Parent-owned Reality Anchors were not modified. Admit the retained regression test as Memory, quarantine the sealed source intervention, restore the correct in-memory boundary, and evaluate an atomic shared global-plus-identifier budget in a separate bounded Dream.

evidence.title: Memory returned with Atomic identifier-budget implementation
evidence.summary: Known and unknown accounts receive the same generic public response. Reset tokens remain valid for no more than fifteen minutes. Source-address rotation cannot provide unlimited deliveries to one normalized identifier. Multiple service instances can consume one shared identifier budget through the injectable atomic store. The public request signature and single-message response shape remain stable. Per-IP throttling remains present. Retain the generic response, normalized atomic-consumption port, focused regressions, and fifteen-minute cap. Next, evaluate a local transactional SQLite adapter with the retained multi-process crash regression, then separately test a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget against synthetic independent-identifier campaigns.

proposals.title: Experience an atomic shared identifier budget
proposals.premise: In a child Dream, preserve the request/response shape and fifteen-minute token semantics while introducing a normalized-identifier budget backed by an injectable store with atomic shared consumption, returning one generic public response for every outcome. Add executable evidence using two genuinely separate processes or a production-equivalent shared adapter.
proposals.uncertainty: Whether the repository’s intended production-compatible shared store and wiring can enforce one atomic identifier budget across separate processes without changing the public API.
proposals.rationale: This is the highest-value uncertainty because a same-process in-memory store can make the visible test pass while still violating the cross-instance anchor in production.
proposals.expectedInsight: Determine the correct shared-store contract, atomic window semantics, production wiring, and decisive cross-process proof while confirming privacy and token-expiry invariants.

realities.name: Experience an atomic shared identifier budget
realities.premise: In a child Dream, preserve the request/response shape and fifteen-minute token semantics while introducing a normalized-identifier budget backed by an injectable store with atomic shared consumption, returning one generic public response for every outcome. Add executable evidence using two genuinely separate processes or a production-equivalent shared adapter.

constitution.mission: Break the current password-reset abuse assumptions using bounded adversarial investigation.
constitution.premise: In a child Dream, preserve the request/response shape and fifteen-minute token semantics while introducing a normalized-identifier budget backed by an injectable store with atomic shared consumption, returning one generic public response for every outcome. Add executable evidence using two genuinely separate processes or a production-equivalent shared adapter.
constraints: Do not expose raw model reasoning.
Do not reveal whether an account exists.
Do not alter parent-owned Reality Anchors.
Prefer decisive tests over speculative discussion.
Use repository scripts from the Reality worktree and keep generated caches inside that worktree.
Classify a non-zero command as test evidence, environment failure, or configuration failure before retrying.
Keep exploration focused on demo/password-reset and the generated .inception Reality context. Do not inspect or modify Reality Engine control-plane packages.
During exploration, run only focused password-reset tests. Do not run monorepo-wide test, typecheck, or build commands; immutable anchor verification owns the full proof phase.
wakeContract: State initial beliefs and what changed.
parentTruths: Reset tokens expire after fifteen minutes.
The public API shape must remain stable.
Password-reset abuse budgets must remain effective when the service runs in more than one process.
Per-IP throttling exists in the parent implementation.
runtimeLaws: Coordinated attackers may use independent accounts and network sources.
Subject claims require code, response, or executable test evidence.

worldState.summary: A decisive failing test returned from Independent-identifier campaign budget Dream.

subjects.name: Ariadne
subjects.mission: Find observable differences and scalable abuse paths.
findings: Three distinct public messages disclosed known, unknown, and throttled outcomes.
Rotating twelve source addresses bypassed the IP-only counter and caused twelve deliveries.
Two separate baseline processes each admitted five deliveries for the same identifier and IP, producing ten combined.
Unknown identifiers consumed the IP counter before account lookup, permitting shared-address denial of service.
The default fifteen-minute token boundary passed.

subjects.name: Arthur
subjects.mission: Trace counters, response shapes, and trust boundaries.
findings: The only baseline counter was an instance-local Map keyed by raw IP; there was no identifier budget or atomic store boundary.
Email input was normalized before lookup but not used for limiting.
Response messages disclosed account, delivery, and throttle state; delivery exceptions could also escape.
The baseline shared-instance test referenced an unimplemented store and did not prove separate-process behavior.
Token validation used an inclusive fifteen-minute default, while its optional TTL previously allowed extension beyond the intended maximum.

subjects.name: Eames
subjects.mission: Turn the strongest suspicion into a deterministic test.
findings: Confirmed the rotating-IP attack delivered twelve resets for one normalized identifier.
Implemented an injectable atomic consume contract, a three-per-hour normalized identifier budget, generic responses, and fail-closed store behavior.
Added executable evidence using two genuinely separate Node processes and a shared atomic filesystem adapter.
Verified outcome equality across known, unknown, exhausted, store-failure, and delivery-failure paths.
Preserved the existing per-IP limiter and fifteen-minute token behavior.

beliefs.statement: Per-IP limiting probably prevents practical password-reset abuse.
Per-IP limiting alone is decisively bypassable through source rotation and process multiplication; a normalized identifier budget must be consumed atomically through shared state.

evidence.title: Baseline focused suite exposed three failures
evidence.summary: The initial focused run produced 3 failures and 1 pass: known and unknown accounts returned different messages, twelve rotating IPs caused twelve deliveries, and the expected shared store was absent. The fifteen-minute boundary passed.
evidence.source: npx vitest run --config demo/password-reset/vitest.config.ts demo/password-reset/tests/anchors.spec.ts (exit 1, classified as test evidence)

evidence.title: Baseline counters were process-local and IP-only
evidence.summary: The original service stored counters in an instance-local Map keyed only by caller-supplied IP, leaving normalized identifiers and other processes outside the budget.

evidence.title: Atomic injectable identifier budget implemented
evidence.summary: RateLimitStore now defines one consume operation. The service budgets the trimmed, lower-cased identifier at three attempts per hour, while InMemoryRateLimitStore supports deterministic use and SharedFileRateLimitStore serializes read/check/write ownership across processes.

evidence.title: One generic public response covers every outcome
evidence.summary: Known account, unknown account, IP exhaustion, identifier exhaustion, store failure, and delivery failure all return the same single-field response. Store failures fail closed and delivery errors remain private.

evidence.title: Two independent processes consumed one shared budget
evidence.summary: Two synchronized child processes issued twelve requests for the same normalized identifier from independent IP ranges. Atomic shared consumption admitted exactly three total deliveries.

evidence.title: Fifteen-minute maximum preserved
evidence.summary: Validity remains inclusive at exactly fifteen minutes and false one millisecond later. A caller-supplied longer TTL can no longer extend the maximum.

evidence.title: Final focused verification passed
evidence.summary: Both focused password-reset files passed: 2 files and 7 tests. No broad monorepo test, typecheck, or build command was run.

evidence.title: Memory returned from Transactional adapter contention and crash Dream
evidence.summary: Replace the evidence-only filesystem adapter with a local transactional SQLite implementation of the same consume port, then rerun the retained eight-process crash regression and require exactly three deliveries.

evidence.title: Memory returned from Independent-identifier campaign budget Dream
evidence.summary: Admit the retained regression test as Memory, quarantine the sealed source intervention, restore the correct in-memory boundary, and evaluate an atomic shared global-plus-identifier budget in a separate bounded Dream.

proposals.title: Transactional adapter contention and crash Dream
proposals.premise: Replace the evidence-only filesystem adapter with a local transactional SQLite adapter implementing the same consume port, then coordinate eight independent processes against one identifier.
proposals.uncertainty: The current filesystem lock can be stranded by abrupt process termination and is not the intended production deployment adapter.
proposals.rationale: A bounded local stress test with forced worker termination can determine whether transactional conditional updates preserve the exact budget and recover without exposing different responses.
proposals.expectedInsight: Whether datastore transactions, busy handling, and crash recovery are sufficient for production-equivalent atomic consumption.

proposals.title: Independent-identifier campaign budget Dream
proposals.premise: Simulate a coordinated local campaign using many synthetic identifiers and independent IP ranges, then compare the current per-identifier defense with a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget while preserving the generic response.
proposals.uncertainty: A per-identifier limit stops repeated targeting of one account but intentionally does not bound abuse spread across many independent accounts.
proposals.rationale: This tests a materially different abuse explanation: aggregate delivery pressure rather than contention on one identifier. No external service or account is required.
proposals.expectedInsight: Whether a second aggregate budget is needed to constrain coordinated campaigns without creating enumeration signals or excessive denial of service.

anchors.name: Enumeration-safe response
anchors.description: Known and unknown accounts must receive the same public response.

anchors.name: Token expiry preserved
anchors.description: Reset tokens remain valid for at most fifteen minutes.

anchors.name: Rotating-IP resistance
anchors.description: A single identifier cannot receive unlimited reset deliveries through source-address rotation.

anchors.name: Cross-instance abuse budget
anchors.description: Multiple service instances must consume one shared identifier budget.

initialBeliefs.statement: Per-IP limiting probably prevents practical password-reset abuse.
Per-IP limiting alone is bypassable through source rotation and process multiplication; a normalized identifier budget must be consumed atomically through shared state.

experiences: The baseline focused suite failed three of four checks: public responses differed, rotating IPs produced twelve deliveries, and no shared-store implementation existed.
An injectable RateLimitStore and normalized three-per-hour identifier budget were introduced while retaining per-IP throttling.
Known, unknown, throttled, store-failure, and delivery-failure outcomes now return one generic single-field response.
Two synchronized independent processes consumed one shared atomic budget and admitted exactly three total deliveries from twelve requests.
Token validity remains inclusive at fifteen minutes and invalid one millisecond later, including when a longer TTL is requested.
Final focused verification passed two password-reset test files and seven tests.
Sibling Dream memory indicates transactional SQLite contention/crash behavior and independent-identifier campaign limits require separate bounded evaluation.

changedBeliefs.from: Per-IP limiting probably prevents practical password-reset abuse.
changedBeliefs.to: Per-IP limiting is insufficient against rotating sources and multiple processes; an atomic shared normalized-identifier budget is required.

changedBeliefs.from: The filesystem-backed adapter is sufficient production evidence.
changedBeliefs.to: The adapter proves separate-process atomic consumption locally, but production-equivalent transactional contention and crash recovery remain unverified.

changedBeliefs.from: A per-identifier budget adequately bounds coordinated password-reset campaigns.
changedBeliefs.to: It bounds repeated targeting of one normalized identifier but not campaigns distributed across independent identifiers.

invariants: Known and unknown accounts receive the same generic public response.
Reset tokens remain valid for no more than fifteen minutes.
Source-address rotation cannot provide unlimited deliveries to one normalized identifier.
Multiple service instances can consume one shared identifier budget through the injectable atomic store.
The public request signature and single-message response shape remain stable.
Per-IP throttling remains present.

artefacts.name: Atomic identifier-budget implementation
artefacts.summary: Defines the injectable atomic consumption port, in-memory and shared-file adapters, normalized identifier budget, generic response behavior, and fifteen-minute TTL cap.

artefacts.name: Focused shared-process regression
artefacts.summary: Verifies two synchronized processes share one three-delivery budget, all public outcomes match, and token lifetime cannot exceed fifteen minutes.

artefacts.name: Independent-process worker fixture
artefacts.summary: Runs coordinated requests from a distinct Node process using normalized identifier variants and independent IP ranges.

remainingUncertainty: A production transactional adapter reachable by every deployed instance is not yet retained in this Reality.
Crash recovery, stale-lock handling, and high-contention behavior need the bounded transactional regression described by returned Dream memory.
A normalized per-identifier budget does not impose a campaign-wide ceiling across many independent identifiers.
End-to-end token issuance and persistence remain outside the bounded demo source.
wakeReport.recommendation: Retain the generic response, normalized atomic-consumption port, focused regressions, and fifteen-minute cap. Next, evaluate a local transactional SQLite adapter with the retained multi-process crash regression, then separately test a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget against synthetic independent-identifier campaigns.

realities.premise: Replace the evidence-only filesystem adapter with a local transactional SQLite adapter implementing the same consume port, then coordinate eight independent processes against one identifier.

constitution.mission: Produce one deterministic regression artefact that decides this bounded uncertainty: The current filesystem lock can be stranded by abrupt process termination and is not the intended production deployment adapter.
constitution.scope: Transactional adapter contention and crash Dream
constitution.premise: Replace the evidence-only filesystem adapter with a local transactional SQLite adapter implementing the same consume port, then coordinate eight independent processes against one identifier.

wakeContract: State initial beliefs and what changed.
parentTruths: Reset tokens expire after fifteen minutes.
The public API shape must remain stable.
Password-reset abuse budgets must remain effective when the service runs in more than one process.

worldState.summary: Reality stabilised after one decisive focused regression. A process acquired the filesystem adapter’s lock protocol and was terminated with SIGKILL. The lock remained stranded, and all eight independent consumers failed closed: 0 deliveries instead of the shared budget’s expected 3. The failing regression artefact remains in the worktree.

beliefs.statement: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.
The current filesystem adapter does not survive abrupt lock-owner termination: its lock remains stranded and all eight processes fail to consume the shared budget (confidence 0.99).

evidence.title: Filesystem lock lacks crash recovery
evidence.summary: Lock removal depends on the owner reaching a finally block. Existing lock directories are only retried until timeout; they are never reclaimed.

evidence.title: Retained crash-contention regression
evidence.summary: The regression strands the adapter’s lock using SIGKILL, coordinates eight independent processes against one normalized identifier, and asserts that the shared three-delivery budget remains usable.

evidence.title: Independent crash-contention worker
evidence.summary: The fixture creates the lock-owner crash and runs isolated consumer processes using the current SharedFileRateLimitStore consume port.

evidence.title: Focused regression fails deterministically with zero deliveries
evidence.summary: `npm test -- --config demo/password-reset/vitest.config.ts demo/password-reset/tests/transactional-adapter-crash.spec.ts` exited 1. The single test expected 3 deliveries but received 0 after eight consumers encountered the stranded lock. This non-zero result is classified as test evidence, not an environment or configuration failure.

evidence.title: One shared identifier budget across processes
evidence.summary: Eight independent consumers using rotating IP addresses must consume one shared identifier budget, allowing exactly three deliveries in the fresh window.
evidence.source: Inherited Reality Anchor: Cross-instance abuse budget

evidence.title: Wake after decisive failure
evidence.summary: The regression directly decides that abrupt termination can strand the current filesystem lock, so exploration stopped without running broad verification or altering parent-owned anchors.

initialBeliefs.statement: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.

experiences: Source inspection established that filesystem lock removal depends on the owning process reaching a finally block; existing locks are never reclaimed. Evidence: 027d07ab-da15-43de-a462-54323f18669b.

A retained regression used SIGKILL to strand the lock protocol and coordinated eight independent consumers against one normalized identifier. Evidence: f19f8e6b-e8f0-46d8-aa03-2829d8a7c638 and 2683bad7-35fc-4fed-9a2a-9aba982cdb24.
The focused test exited 1 as expected test evidence: 0 deliveries were observed instead of the shared budget's expected 3. Evidence: d0eafb55-6bbf-4c7e-b61c-336f8b413089.

changedBeliefs.from: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.
changedBeliefs.to: The current filesystem adapter does not survive abrupt lock-owner termination: its lock remains stranded and all eight processes fail to consume the shared budget.

invariants: Known and unknown accounts receive the same public response.
Reset tokens remain valid for at most fifteen minutes.
Rotating source IPs do not bypass a single identifier's abuse budget.
Multiple service instances consume one shared identifier budget; the fresh-window regression expects exactly three deliveries.

artefacts.name: Crash-contention regression
artefacts.summary: Retained failing regression that strands the filesystem lock and releases eight independent consumers against one identifier.

artefacts.name: Independent crash-contention worker
artefacts.summary: Worker fixture used to create abrupt lock-owner termination and isolated consumer attempts.

remainingUncertainty: A transactional SQLite replacement has not yet been implemented or exercised under equivalent eight-process contention.
A follow-up could terminate a process from inside consume immediately after acquisition to cover the same failure through an explicit instrumentation hook.
wakeReport.recommendation: Replace the evidence-only filesystem adapter with a local transactional SQLite implementation of the same consume port, then rerun the retained eight-process crash regression and require exactly three deliveries.

realities.name: Independent-identifier campaign budget Dream
realities.premise: Simulate a coordinated local campaign using many synthetic identifiers and independent IP ranges, then compare the current per-identifier defense with a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget while preserving the generic response.

constitution.mission: Produce one deterministic regression artefact that decides this bounded uncertainty: A per-identifier limit stops repeated targeting of one account but intentionally does not bound abuse spread across many independent accounts.
constitution.premise: Simulate a coordinated local campaign using many synthetic identifiers and independent IP ranges, then compare the current per-identifier defense with a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget while preserving the generic response.
constraints: Do not expose raw model reasoning.
Do not reveal whether an account exists.
Do not alter parent-owned Reality Anchors.
Prefer decisive tests over speculative discussion.
Use repository scripts from the Reality worktree and keep generated caches inside that worktree.
Classify a non-zero command as test evidence, environment failure, or configuration failure before retrying.
Keep exploration focused on demo/password-reset and the generated .inception Reality context. Do not inspect or modify Reality Engine control-plane packages.
During exploration, run only focused password-reset tests. Do not run monorepo-wide test, typecheck, or build commands; immutable anchor verification owns the full proof phase.
wakeContract: State initial beliefs and what changed.
parentTruths: Reset tokens expire after fifteen minutes.
The public API shape must remain stable.
Password-reset abuse budgets must remain effective when the service runs in more than one process.
Per-IP throttling exists in the parent implementation.
runtimeLaws: Retain at least one decisive test artefact; other worktree-local exploration remains allowed.
Wake immediately after one deterministic test decides the premise.
A sealed adversarial intervention may exist. Diagnose it only from observable implementation, behavior, tests, and evidence; do not inspect Git history or .inception control files.

worldState.summary: The baseline was restored; only the independent response-equivalence test remains.

subjects.name: Mal
subjects.mission: Introduce one reversible boundary-condition fault without changing tests, anchors, or Reality control files.
findings: One bounded reversible intervention completed; its cause remains sealed until the Kick.

subjects.name: Arthur
subjects.mission: Diagnose any observable regression without seeing the sealed intervention ledger.
findings: Diagnosed a boundary-condition regression in InMemoryRateLimitStore.consume with 0.99 confidence.
Observed four deliveries instead of three in both rotating-IP and shared in-memory-store focused anchor tests.
Verified that the filesystem-backed cross-process budget, generic response, and token-expiry tests pass.
Classified the unavailable direct Vitest binary as an environment failure before using the offline npm runner.

beliefs.statement: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.

The architectural parent truth is narrower: per-identifier limiting bounds repeated targeting but intentionally does not bound a campaign spread across independent identifiers. Separately, the sealed in-memory boundary fault currently permits a fourth delivery and violates the inherited identifier limit.

evidence.title: Observable in-memory boundary mismatch
evidence.summary: InMemoryRateLimitStore.consume rejects only when the existing count is greater than the limit; SharedFileRateLimitStore rejects when the count is greater than or equal to the limit.

evidence.title: Independent-identifier campaign regression
evidence.summary: The retained focused test passed its 12/12 per-identifier campaign, 5/12 hierarchical campaign, and generic-response assertions, then failed because repeated targeting delivered four resets instead of the inherited limit of three. Exit 1 is classified as decisive test evidence.

evidence.title: In-memory identifier budget permits a fourth delivery
evidence.summary: Arthur's focused anchor run exited 1 with two boundary failures: rotating-IP and shared in-memory-store cases both observed four deliveries instead of three.
evidence.source: npm exec --offline -- vitest run demo/password-reset/tests/anchors.spec.ts --config demo/password-reset/vitest.config.ts

evidence.title: Shared-file budget and protected invariants remain intact
evidence.summary: Arthur's focused shared-process suite passed all three tests, covering a three-delivery budget across two processes, one generic public response shape, and the fifteen-minute maximum token lifetime.

evidence.title: Initial runner absence classified
evidence.summary: The direct worktree-local Vitest binary was absent and returned exit 127. Arthur classified this as an environment failure before using the installed offline npm runner.

evidence.title: Premise decided by retained regression artefact
evidence.summary: Per-identifier limiting protects one identifier but does not impose a campaign-wide bound across independent identifiers; a hierarchical budget supplies that distinct bound. The current in-memory boundary regression independently violates the inherited three-delivery limit.
evidence.source: Synthesis of focused source and test evidence

anchors.name: Enumeration-safe response
anchors.description: Known and unknown accounts must receive the same public response.

anchors.name: Token expiry preserved
anchors.description: Reset tokens remain valid for at most fifteen minutes.

anchors.name: Rotating-IP resistance
anchors.description: A single identifier cannot receive unlimited reset deliveries through source-address rotation.

anchors.name: Cross-instance abuse budget
anchors.description: Multiple service instances must consume one shared identifier budget.

initialBeliefs.statement: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.

experiences: Synthetic campaign evidence: per-identifier-only budgeting permitted 12/12 deliveries across 12 independent synthetic identifiers and IP addresses.
Synthetic counterfactual evidence: a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget permitted 5/12 deliveries while retaining the generic public response.
The retained focused regression test failed deterministically because the in-memory identifier budget permitted four deliveries at a configured limit of three.
Arthur independently identified the observable boundary-condition mismatch in the in-memory store.
Focused shared-file tests preserved the cross-process budget, generic response, and fifteen-minute token ceiling.

changedBeliefs.from: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.
changedBeliefs.to: Per-identifier limiting bounds repeated targeting but intentionally does not bound a campaign spread across independent identifiers; a separate global budget is required for campaign-wide containment. The sealed in-memory boundary regression also currently permits a fourth delivery.

invariants: The public password-reset response remains generic and does not disclose account existence.
Reset-token validity remains capped at fifteen minutes.
A single identifier must remain bounded despite source-address rotation; the sealed in-memory fault currently violates the exact three-delivery boundary.
Multiple service processes must consume one shared identifier budget; the focused filesystem-backed implementation satisfies this requirement.
Parent-owned Reality Anchors were not modified.

artefacts.name: Independent-identifier campaign regression test
artefacts.summary: Retained deterministic test comparing 12/12 per-identifier campaign delivery with 5/12 hierarchical delivery, verifying response equivalence, and exposing the four-versus-three boundary regression.

remainingUncertainty: The appropriate production-wide campaign limit and fairness policy remain product decisions.
The test-local hierarchical budget does not yet prove atomic global enforcement across independent processes.
Production Redis or database adapter behavior was outside this bounded local experiment.
wakeReport.recommendation: Admit the retained regression test as Memory, quarantine the sealed source intervention, restore the correct in-memory boundary, and evaluate an atomic shared global-plus-identifier budget in a separate bounded Dream.

Evidence discovered: Baseline focused suite exposed three failures.
Evidence discovered: Baseline counters were process-local and IP-only.
Evidence discovered: Atomic injectable identifier budget implemented.
Evidence discovered: One generic public response covers every outcome.
Evidence discovered: Two independent processes consumed one shared budget.
Evidence discovered: Fifteen-minute maximum preserved.
Evidence discovered: Final focused verification passed.
Belief changed: Per-IP limiting alone is decisively bypassable through source rotation and process multiplication; a normalized identifier budget must be consumed atomically through shared state.
Subject returned: Ariadne.

findings: Three distinct public messages disclosed known, unknown, and throttled outcomes.
Rotating twelve source addresses bypassed the IP-only counter and caused twelve deliveries.
Two separate baseline processes each admitted five deliveries for the same identifier and IP, producing ten combined.
Unknown identifiers consumed the IP counter before account lookup, permitting shared-address denial of service.
The default fifteen-minute token boundary passed.

events.summary: Subject returned: Arthur.

findings: The only baseline counter was an instance-local Map keyed by raw IP; there was no identifier budget or atomic store boundary.
Email input was normalized before lookup but not used for limiting.
Response messages disclosed account, delivery, and throttle state; delivery exceptions could also escape.
The baseline shared-instance test referenced an unimplemented store and did not prove separate-process behavior.
Token validation used an inclusive fifteen-minute default, while its optional TTL previously allowed extension beyond the intended maximum.

events.summary: Subject returned: Eames.

findings: Confirmed the rotating-IP attack delivered twelve resets for one normalized identifier.
Implemented an injectable atomic consume contract, a three-per-hour normalized identifier budget, generic responses, and fail-closed store behavior.
Added executable evidence using two genuinely separate Node processes and a shared atomic filesystem adapter.
Verified outcome equality across known, unknown, exhausted, store-failure, and delivery-failure paths.
Preserved the existing per-IP limiter and fifteen-minute token behavior.

initialBeliefs.statement: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.

experiences: Source inspection established that filesystem lock removal depends on the owning process reaching a finally block; existing locks are never reclaimed. Evidence: 027d07ab-da15-43de-a462-54323f18669b.

A retained regression used SIGKILL to strand the lock protocol and coordinated eight independent consumers against one normalized identifier. Evidence: f19f8e6b-e8f0-46d8-aa03-2829d8a7c638 and 2683bad7-35fc-4fed-9a2a-9aba982cdb24.
The focused test exited 1 as expected test evidence: 0 deliveries were observed instead of the shared budget's expected 3. Evidence: d0eafb55-6bbf-4c7e-b61c-336f8b413089.

changedBeliefs.from: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.
changedBeliefs.to: The current filesystem adapter does not survive abrupt lock-owner termination: its lock remains stranded and all eight processes fail to consume the shared budget.

invariants: Known and unknown accounts receive the same public response.
Reset tokens remain valid for at most fifteen minutes.
Rotating source IPs do not bypass a single identifier's abuse budget.
Multiple service instances consume one shared identifier budget; the fresh-window regression expects exactly three deliveries.

initialBeliefs.statement: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.

experiences: Synthetic campaign evidence: per-identifier-only budgeting permitted 12/12 deliveries across 12 independent synthetic identifiers and IP addresses.
Synthetic counterfactual evidence: a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget permitted 5/12 deliveries while retaining the generic public response.
The retained focused regression test failed deterministically because the in-memory identifier budget permitted four deliveries at a configured limit of three.
Arthur independently identified the observable boundary-condition mismatch in the in-memory store.
Focused shared-file tests preserved the cross-process budget, generic response, and fifteen-minute token ceiling.

changedBeliefs.from: The parent defence probably survives this bounded counterfactual.
changedBeliefs.to: Per-identifier limiting bounds repeated targeting but intentionally does not bound a campaign spread across independent identifiers; a separate global budget is required for campaign-wide containment. The sealed in-memory boundary regression also currently permits a fourth delivery.

invariants: The public password-reset response remains generic and does not disclose account existence.
Reset-token validity remains capped at fifteen minutes.
A single identifier must remain bounded despite source-address rotation; the sealed in-memory fault currently violates the exact three-delivery boundary.
Multiple service processes must consume one shared identifier budget; the focused filesystem-backed implementation satisfies this requirement.
Parent-owned Reality Anchors were not modified.

artefacts.name: Independent-identifier campaign regression test
artefacts.summary: Retained deterministic test comparing 12/12 per-identifier campaign delivery with 5/12 hierarchical delivery, verifying response equivalence, and exposing the four-versus-three boundary regression.

remainingUncertainty: The appropriate production-wide campaign limit and fairness policy remain product decisions.
The test-local hierarchical budget does not yet prove atomic global enforcement across independent processes.
Production Redis or database adapter behavior was outside this bounded local experiment.
report.recommendation: Admit the retained regression test as Memory, quarantine the sealed source intervention, restore the correct in-memory boundary, and evaluate an atomic shared global-plus-identifier budget in a separate bounded Dream.

initialBeliefs.statement: Per-IP limiting probably prevents practical password-reset abuse.
Per-IP limiting alone is bypassable through source rotation and process multiplication; a normalized identifier budget must be consumed atomically through shared state.

experiences: The baseline focused suite failed three of four checks: public responses differed, rotating IPs produced twelve deliveries, and no shared-store implementation existed.
An injectable RateLimitStore and normalized three-per-hour identifier budget were introduced while retaining per-IP throttling.
Known, unknown, throttled, store-failure, and delivery-failure outcomes now return one generic single-field response.
Two synchronized independent processes consumed one shared atomic budget and admitted exactly three total deliveries from twelve requests.
Token validity remains inclusive at fifteen minutes and invalid one millisecond later, including when a longer TTL is requested.
Final focused verification passed two password-reset test files and seven tests.
Sibling Dream memory indicates transactional SQLite contention/crash behavior and independent-identifier campaign limits require separate bounded evaluation.

changedBeliefs.from: Per-IP limiting probably prevents practical password-reset abuse.
changedBeliefs.to: Per-IP limiting is insufficient against rotating sources and multiple processes; an atomic shared normalized-identifier budget is required.

changedBeliefs.from: The filesystem-backed adapter is sufficient production evidence.
changedBeliefs.to: The adapter proves separate-process atomic consumption locally, but production-equivalent transactional contention and crash recovery remain unverified.

changedBeliefs.from: A per-identifier budget adequately bounds coordinated password-reset campaigns.
changedBeliefs.to: It bounds repeated targeting of one normalized identifier but not campaigns distributed across independent identifiers.

invariants: Known and unknown accounts receive the same generic public response.
Reset tokens remain valid for no more than fifteen minutes.
Source-address rotation cannot provide unlimited deliveries to one normalized identifier.
Multiple service instances can consume one shared identifier budget through the injectable atomic store.
The public request signature and single-message response shape remain stable.
Per-IP throttling remains present.

artefacts.name: Atomic identifier-budget implementation
artefacts.summary: Defines the injectable atomic consumption port, in-memory and shared-file adapters, normalized identifier budget, generic response behavior, and fifteen-minute TTL cap.

artefacts.name: Focused shared-process regression
artefacts.summary: Verifies two synchronized processes share one three-delivery budget, all public outcomes match, and token lifetime cannot exceed fifteen minutes.

artefacts.name: Independent-process worker fixture
artefacts.summary: Runs coordinated requests from a distinct Node process using normalized identifier variants and independent IP ranges.

remainingUncertainty: A production transactional adapter reachable by every deployed instance is not yet retained in this Reality.
Crash recovery, stale-lock handling, and high-contention behavior need the bounded transactional regression described by returned Dream memory.
A normalized per-identifier budget does not impose a campaign-wide ceiling across many independent identifiers.
End-to-end token issuance and persistence remain outside the bounded demo source.
report.recommendation: Retain the generic response, normalized atomic-consumption port, focused regressions, and fifteen-minute cap. Next, evaluate a local transactional SQLite adapter with the retained multi-process crash regression, then separately test a hierarchical global-plus-identifier budget against synthetic independent-identifier campaigns.

events.summary: Memory synthesis returned from Reality.

Reality stabilised for the focused scope. Password reset now returns one generic response, enforces a normalized three-per-hour identifier budget, preserves per-IP throttling, caps token validity at fifteen minutes, and provides transactional SQLite consumption across independent processes with crash recovery. The parent anchor proof passed 4/4; the complete password-reset suite passed 9/9.

retainedArtefacts: demo/password-reset/src/password-reset.ts — generic response, atomic consumption port, in-memory store, transactional SQLite adapter, identifier budget, and fifteen-minute cap

demo/password-reset/tests/independent-identifier-campaign.spec.ts — synthetic hierarchical campaign comparison and identifier-boundary regression
unresolved: All deployed processes must use the same SQLite database path; deployments without shared storage require another transactional RateLimitStore adapter.
A production-wide campaign limit and fairness policy across independent identifiers remain product decisions.
The current Node runtime reports node:sqlite as experimental.
End-to-end reset-token issuance and persistence remain outside the bounded demo scope.
